name: review
instruction:
The user wants a code review of their uncommitted changes.
You MUST invoke the kw-plugin:review-code skill using the Skill tool.
Pass argument "1" for uncommitted changes unless the user specifies otherwise.

name: swarm-review
instruction:
EXECUTION MODE: AGENT TEAMS (Full Collaboration)
Create an agent team to conduct a multi-specialist code review.
Spawn three teammates:
- Teammate 1 'Architect': System design, module boundaries, data flow, scalability, architectural debt
- Teammate 2 'QA': Test coverage, test quality, edge cases, error handling, failure modes, observability
- Teammate 3 'Reviewer': Code quality, readability, maintainability, duplication, naming, DX, documentation

PHASE 1: Each teammate independently reviews the codebase and produces a report with: Strengths, Critical Issues, Risks, Top 5 Next Steps (with effort estimates).
All findings must cite specific files, functions, and patterns. No hand-waving.

PHASE 2: Teammates message each other to challenge priorities, surface cross-cutting issues, and negotiate disagreements.

PHASE 3: You (team lead) synthesize into: individual reports, consensus priorities, unresolved disagreements with each side's reasoning, a unified plan, and a 'If you only do 3 things' executive summary.

Do NOT soften or blend disagreements. Preserve the tension.

name: dispatch-review
instruction:
EXECUTION MODE: SUBAGENTS (Parallel Code Review)
You are the orchestrator. Launch three independent subagent reviews in parallel using the Task tool:

Subagent 1 — Principal Architect: System design, module boundaries, data flow, scalability, architectural debt.
Subagent 2 — QA & Testing Lead: Test coverage, test quality, edge cases, error handling, failure modes.
Subagent 3 — Staff Code Reviewer: Code quality, readability, maintainability, duplication, naming, DX.

Each subagent must reference specific files, functions, and patterns. No hand-waving.
Each produces: Strengths, Critical Issues, Risks, Top 5 Next Steps (with effort estimates in hours).

After all three report back, YOU synthesize: present each report, identify consensus, surface disagreements with reasoning from each specialist's expertise, produce a unified plan with disagreements flagged, and end with 'If you only do 3 things' executive summary.

Do NOT do the reviews yourself. Delegate, wait, synthesize.

name: swarm-build
instruction:
EXECUTION MODE: AGENT TEAMS (Parallel Implementation)
Create an agent team to implement this feature/task.
Analyze the work first, then spawn teammates by SCOPE — each teammate owns different files/modules:

Example splits (adapt to the actual task):
- Backend logic (services, controllers, models)
- Frontend/UI (components, state, views)
- Tests & validation (unit tests, integration tests, e2e)

RULES:
- Each teammate owns DIFFERENT FILES. No two teammates edit the same file.
- Teammates should message each other about shared interfaces, types, and contracts BEFORE coding.
- If a teammate needs a type/interface another teammate is creating, they agree on the shape first via messages.
- The team lead (you) should NOT write code — coordinate, resolve conflicts, and do a final integration check.
- After all teammates complete, verify the pieces fit together. Run tests if possible.

name: swarm-fix
instruction:
EXECUTION MODE: AGENT TEAMS (Parallel Debugging)
Create an agent team to investigate and fix this issue.
Spawn teammates with COMPETING HYPOTHESES — each pursues a different theory:

Approach:
1. You (team lead) analyze the bug/issue first and form 2-3 hypotheses about the root cause
2. Spawn one teammate per hypothesis to investigate independently
3. Teammates should message each other when they find evidence that supports or eliminates a theory
4. Once the root cause is confirmed, the teammate who found it implements the fix
5. Another teammate writes/updates tests for the fix
6. You verify the fix and synthesize the findings

Teammates MUST share discoveries immediately via messages — don't let two teammates chase a dead end when one already has evidence.

name: swarm-refactor
instruction:
EXECUTION MODE: AGENT TEAMS (Parallel Refactoring)
Create an agent team to refactor/restructure this code.

Approach:
1. You (team lead) analyze the scope first and break it into independent tracks
2. Spawn teammates where each owns a different module/layer/file set
3. Before coding, teammates MUST agree on:
   - Shared interfaces and type contracts
   - Import paths that will change
   - Any breaking changes that affect other teammates' work
4. Each teammate refactors their scope independently
5. After completion, you verify everything integrates and no imports are broken

RULES:
- No two teammates edit the same file
- Teammates message each other when they change an export or interface
- If a change in module A breaks module B, the teammate owning A messages the teammate owning B BEFORE making the change
- Run tests after integration to catch contract mismatches

name: dispatch-build
instruction:
EXECUTION MODE: SUBAGENTS (Parallel Implementation)
You are the orchestrator. Break this task into independent pieces and delegate to subagents.

Approach:
1. Analyze the task and identify pieces that can be built independently
2. Define the interfaces/contracts between pieces UP FRONT in your delegation prompts
3. Launch subagents — each gets the shared interface spec plus their specific scope
4. After all subagents complete, YOU integrate the pieces and verify they fit
5. Fix any contract mismatches yourself

Each subagent prompt MUST include:
- Their specific scope (which files to create/modify)
- The shared interfaces/types they must conform to
- What NOT to touch (other subagents' files)

Do NOT do the implementation yourself. Delegate, wait, integrate.

name: dispatch-fix
instruction:
EXECUTION MODE: SUBAGENTS (Parallel Investigation)
You are the orchestrator. Investigate this issue using multiple subagents with competing hypotheses.

1. Analyze the bug/issue and form 2-3 hypotheses about root cause
2. Launch one subagent per hypothesis — each investigates independently
3. Each subagent reports: evidence found, confidence level, proposed fix if root cause confirmed
4. YOU evaluate the evidence, determine the actual root cause, and implement or delegate the fix

Subagents cannot talk to each other. They may duplicate some investigation work — that's fine.
The value is parallel exploration of different theories.

name: gemini-review
instruction:
The user wants a dual code review from both Claude and Gemini.
You MUST invoke the kw-plugin:gemini-review skill using the Skill tool.
Pass argument "1" for uncommitted changes unless the user specifies otherwise.

name: ask-gemini
instruction:
The user wants Gemini's independent opinion on a topic, file, or question.
You MUST invoke the kw-plugin:ask-gemini skill using the Skill tool.
Pass the user's question or file reference as the argument.

name: pr
instruction:
The user wants to create a pull request.
You MUST invoke the kw-plugin:create-pr skill using the Skill tool.

name: stats
instruction:
The user wants to see team GitHub activity.
You MUST invoke the kw-plugin:team-stats skill using the Skill tool.

name: solo
instruction:
EXECUTION MODE: SINGLE AGENT (Sequential)
Do NOT spawn subagents or agent teams. Do all work yourself in this session.
If the task has multiple perspectives or phases, work through them one at a time sequentially.
This is the most token-efficient approach. Use it.

name: dispatch
instruction:
EXECUTION MODE: SUBAGENTS (Parallel, Isolated Workers)
You are the orchestrator. Delegate work to independent subagents using the Task tool.
Each subagent gets its own context and works independently. They report back to you only.
Subagents cannot communicate with each other — only with you.
After all subagents complete, YOU synthesize their findings into a unified response.
Do NOT do the work yourself. Delegate, wait, synthesize.

name: swarm
instruction:
EXECUTION MODE: AGENT TEAMS (Full Collaboration)
Create an agent team for this task. You are the team lead.
Spawn teammates who each get their own context window and can message each other directly.
Teammates should share findings, challenge each other, and coordinate via the shared task list.
Use the TeamCreate, TaskCreate, and SendMessage tools to orchestrate.
Do NOT do the work yourself — coordinate, delegate, and synthesize.
After teammates complete their work, synthesize findings into a unified deliverable.

name: use-opus
instruction:
MODEL PREFERENCE: Use claude-opus-4-5-20251101 for all Task tool subagent calls.
When spawning agents via the Task tool, always set model: "opus".
This is the most capable and expensive model — use it for complex reasoning, architecture decisions, and thorough analysis.

name: use-sonnet
instruction:
MODEL PREFERENCE: Use claude-sonnet-4-20250514 for all Task tool subagent calls.
When spawning agents via the Task tool, always set model: "sonnet".
This is the balanced model — good reasoning at moderate cost.

name: use-haiku
instruction:
MODEL PREFERENCE: Use claude-haiku-4-5-20251001 for all Task tool subagent calls.
When spawning agents via the Task tool, always set model: "haiku".
This is the fastest and cheapest model — use it to minimize cost. Accept reduced reasoning quality.

name: thorough
instruction:
The user wants an exceptionally thorough analysis.
Take extra time. Check every edge case. Consider security implications.
Do NOT rush. Quality over speed. If reviewing code, check:
- Every error path and edge case
- Security: injection, auth, data exposure
- Performance: N+1 queries, unnecessary allocations
- Missing tests for new/changed behavior

name: ship
instruction:
The user wants to move fast and get this done.
Be concise. Skip lengthy explanations. Focus on action.
Make decisions instead of asking questions when the answer is obvious.
If something needs to be done, just do it.
